cards.title: Clinical Scoring Systems — Overview & Priority List

alerts.label: Why Scoring Systems Matter
alerts.text: Validated scoring systems replace subjective clinical gestalt with objective, reproducible risk stratification. They guide: disposition decisions (admit vs discharge vs ICU), treatment intensity, communication between providers, and medicolegal documentation. Every BNYS practitioner should know the scores most relevant to their patient population.

rows: Community pneumonia severity + need for hospitalisation
Rapid bedside sepsis screen — no labs needed
≥2/3: high risk sepsis → full SOFA + escalate care
SOFA ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction. Mortality ↑ with each point.
≤8: severe TBI — intubation consideration. ≤13: significant impairment.
Acute chest pain — MACE risk at 6 weeks
0–3: low (1.7% MACE) — discharge with follow-up. 4–6: moderate — observe. ≥7: high — admission + angiography.
Class A (5–6): compensated. Class B (7–9): moderate. Class C (10–15): decompensated — poor surgical candidate.
End-stage liver disease mortality + transplant listing
MELD <10: low mortality. 10–19: moderate. ≥20: high — transplant evaluation. ≥40: very high hospital mortality.
<2: low — D-dimer to exclude. ≥2: moderate — ultrasound. >6: high — anticoagulate empirically pending imaging.
<2: low. 2–6: moderate. >6: high — CT-PA directly without D-dimer.
7–10: normal. 4–6: moderate compromise. <4: severe — immediate resuscitation.
10–14: at risk. 15–19: high risk. ≥20: very high risk. Initiate preventive measures accordingly.
Upper GI bleed — need for intervention
0: very low risk — may discharge. ≥1: endoscopy needed. Higher score = higher need for transfusion/surgery.

cards._legacyHtml:
Why Scoring Systems Matter
Validated scoring systems replace subjective clinical gestalt with objective, reproducible risk stratification. They guide: disposition decisions (admit vs discharge vs ICU), treatment intensity, communication between providers, and medicolegal documentation. Every BNYS practitioner should know the scores most relevant to their patient population.
ScoreDomainPurposeAction Threshold
CURB-65RespiratoryCommunity pneumonia severity + need for hospitalisation0–1: home treatment. 2: hospital. ≥3: ICU consideration
qSOFASepsisRapid bedside sepsis screen — no labs needed≥2/3: high risk sepsis → full SOFA + escalate care
SOFASepsis/ICUOrgan dysfunction quantification in ICUSOFA ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction. Mortality ↑ with each point.
GCSNeurologyLevel of consciousness quantification≤8: severe TBI — intubation consideration. ≤13: significant impairment.
NIHSSNeurologyStroke severity — guides thrombolysis decision0: no deficit. 1–4: minor. 5–15: moderate. ≥16: severe.
HEART ScoreCardiologyAcute chest pain — MACE risk at 6 weeks0–3: low (1.7% MACE) — discharge with follow-up. 4–6: moderate — observe. ≥7: high — admission + angiography.
Child-PughHepatologyCirrhosis severity — prognosis + surgical riskClass A (5–6): compensated. Class B (7–9): moderate. Class C (10–15): decompensated — poor surgical candidate.
MELD ScoreHepatologyEnd-stage liver disease mortality + transplant listingMELD <10: low mortality. 10–19: moderate. ≥20: high — transplant evaluation. ≥40: very high hospital mortality.
Wells Score (DVT)VascularPre-test probability for DVT<2: low — D-dimer to exclude. ≥2: moderate — ultrasound. >6: high — anticoagulate empirically pending imaging.
Wells Score (PE)VascularPre-test probability for pulmonary embolism<2: low. 2–6: moderate. >6: high — CT-PA directly without D-dimer.
CHA₂DS₂-VAScCardiologyAF stroke risk — anticoagulation decision0 (male) / 1 (female): no anticoagulation. ≥2: anticoagulate. ≥3: strong indication.
APGAR ScorePaediatricsNeonatal wellbeing at 1 and 5 minutes7–10: normal. 4–6: moderate compromise. <4: severe — immediate resuscitation.
Waterlow ScoreNursing/WoundPressure ulcer risk assessment10–14: at risk. 15–19: high risk. ≥20: very high risk. Initiate preventive measures accordingly.
Glasgow-Blatchford ScoreGastroenterologyUpper GI bleed — need for intervention0: very low risk — may discharge. ≥1: endoscopy needed. Higher score = higher need for transfusion/surgery.

cards.title: CURB-65 & Sepsis Scores — Bedside Application

steps.title: CURB-65 — Community Pneumonia Severity
steps.text: C = Confusion (new onset, AMT ≤8). U = Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >19 mg/dL). R = RR ≥30/min. B = BP systolic <90 OR diastolic ≤60 mmHg. 65 = Age ≥65. Score: 0–1 = low mortality (1.5%) — home. 2 = moderate (9.2%) — hospital admission. 3–5 = high (22%) — consider ICU. Note: clinical judgement always overrides score — oxygen saturation <92%, bilateral involvement, comorbidities, social factors all influence decision.

steps.title: qSOFA — Quick Sepsis Organ Failure Assessment
steps.text: 3 bedside criteria (no labs needed): (1) Altered mental status (GCS <15). (2) RR ≥22/min. (3) Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg. Score ≥2 = high risk for sepsis-related organ dysfunction and in-hospital mortality. Sensitivity 70% but high specificity — positive qSOFA = escalate immediately: lactate, blood cultures ×2, IV access, fluid challenge, antibiotics within 1 hour. Start Sepsis 1-Hour Bundle.

steps.title: SOFA Score Components (ICU Use)
steps.text: 6 organ systems, 0–4 points each (max 24). Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂), Coagulation (platelets), Liver (bilirubin), Cardiovascular (MAP or vasopressor dose), CNS (GCS), Renal (creatinine or urine output). SOFA ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction = sepsis diagnosis (Sepsis-3 definition). Sequential SOFA trend: improving score = positive prognosis; worsening = mortality risk ↑ dramatically.

cards._legacyHtml:
C
CURB-65 — Community Pneumonia Severity C = Confusion (new onset, AMT ≤8). U = Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >19 mg/dL). R = RR ≥30/min. B = BP systolic <90 OR diastolic ≤60 mmHg. 65 = Age ≥65. Score: 0–1 = low mortality (1.5%) — home. 2 = moderate (9.2%) — hospital admission. 3–5 = high (22%) — consider ICU. Note: clinical judgement always overrides score — oxygen saturation <92%, bilateral involvement, comorbidities, social factors all influence decision.
q
qSOFA — Quick Sepsis Organ Failure Assessment 3 bedside criteria (no labs needed): (1) Altered mental status (GCS <15). (2) RR ≥22/min. (3) Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg. Score ≥2 = high risk for sepsis-related organ dysfunction and in-hospital mortality. Sensitivity 70% but high specificity — positive qSOFA = escalate immediately: lactate, blood cultures ×2, IV access, fluid challenge, antibiotics within 1 hour. Start Sepsis 1-Hour Bundle.
S
SOFA Score Components (ICU Use) 6 organ systems, 0–4 points each (max 24). Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂), Coagulation (platelets), Liver (bilirubin), Cardiovascular (MAP or vasopressor dose), CNS (GCS), Renal (creatinine or urine output). SOFA ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction = sepsis diagnosis (Sepsis-3 definition). Sequential SOFA trend: improving score = positive prognosis; worsening = mortality risk ↑ dramatically.

cards.title: HEART Score & Cardiac Scoring

alerts.label: HEART Score Action
alerts.text: 0–3: Low risk (1.7% 6-week MACE) — early discharge with outpatient follow-up. 4–6: Moderate — observe, serial troponin, stress test before discharge. ≥7: High risk (50–65% MACE) — admit, cardiology referral, consider angiography.

rows: Significant ST deviation, LBBB, LVH, early repol, paced rhythm
≥3 risk factors OR history of atherosclerotic disease
≥3: high bleeding risk — does NOT contraindicate anticoagulation, guides risk-benefit discussion
I: no HF (6% mortality). IV: cardiogenic shock (80% mortality)

cards._legacyHtml:
HEART Component0 Points1 Point2 Points
HistorySlightly suspiciousModerately suspiciousHighly suspicious for ACS
ECGNormalNon-specific repolarisation disturbanceSignificant ST deviation, LBBB, LVH, early repol, paced rhythm
Age<45 years45–65 years>65 years
Risk factorsNo known risk factors1–2 risk factors OR DM/obesity≥3 risk factors OR history of atherosclerotic disease
Troponin≤Normal limit1–3× normal limit>3× normal limit
HEART Score Action
0–3: Low risk (1.7% 6-week MACE) — early discharge with outpatient follow-up. 4–6: Moderate — observe, serial troponin, stress test before discharge. ≥7: High risk (50–65% MACE) — admit, cardiology referral, consider angiography.
ScoreSystemWhat It CalculatesKey Numbers
TIMI (STEMI)Cardiology30-day mortality risk post-STEMIScore 0: 0.8% mortality. Score 8: 35.9% mortality
GRACECardiologyIn-hospital + 6-month mortality in ACSLow <109; Intermediate 109–140; High >140
HAS-BLEDCardiology1-year major bleeding risk on anticoagulation (AF)≥3: high bleeding risk — does NOT contraindicate anticoagulation, guides risk-benefit discussion
Killip ClassCardiologyClinical heart failure severity post-MII: no HF (6% mortality). IV: cardiogenic shock (80% mortality)

cards.title: Liver, Renal & Neurological Scoring

rows: A (5–6): compensated. B (7–9): moderate. C (10–15): decompensated
Surgical risk, prognosis, medication dosing in cirrhosis. Class C: 1-year survival 45%
Scale 6–40. Each point ↑ = ↑ 3-month mortality. ≥15: transplant benefit. ≥25: high urgency.
Liver transplant listing priority. Better than Child-Pugh for predicting short-term mortality.
11 items: consciousness, gaze, visual field, facial palsy, motor (arm/leg), ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, extinction
Guides thrombolysis decision (eligibility if symptoms severe enough). Prognosis predictor. Serial assessment for improvement.
Eye response, motor response, brainstem reflexes, respiration (0–4 each)
Max 16 = alert normal. 0 = brain death criteria
Superior to GCS for intubated patients (GCS verbal = 1 for intubated, loses sensitivity). ICU use.
Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration (0/1/2 each)
Neonatal resuscitation guide. Document at 1 min (resuscitation need) and 5 min (response to resuscitation). <7 at 5 min → resuscitate + reassess.

cards._legacyHtml:
ScoreVariablesInterpretationClinical Use
Child-PughEncephalopathy (0/1/2), ascites (none/mild/severe), bilirubin, albumin, PTA (5–6): compensated. B (7–9): moderate. C (10–15): decompensatedSurgical risk, prognosis, medication dosing in cirrhosis. Class C: 1-year survival 45%
MELDCreatinine, bilirubin, INR (formula-based)Scale 6–40. Each point ↑ = ↑ 3-month mortality. ≥15: transplant benefit. ≥25: high urgency.Liver transplant listing priority. Better than Child-Pugh for predicting short-term mortality.
NIHSS11 items: consciousness, gaze, visual field, facial palsy, motor (arm/leg), ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, extinction0: normal. 1–4: minor. 5–15: moderate. 16–20: moderate-severe. ≥21: severeGuides thrombolysis decision (eligibility if symptoms severe enough). Prognosis predictor. Serial assessment for improvement.
FOUR ScoreEye response, motor response, brainstem reflexes, respiration (0–4 each)Max 16 = alert normal. 0 = brain death criteriaSuperior to GCS for intubated patients (GCS verbal = 1 for intubated, loses sensitivity). ICU use.
APGAR ScoreAppearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration (0/1/2 each)7–10: reassuring. 4–6: moderately depressed. 0–3: severely depressedNeonatal resuscitation guide. Document at 1 min (resuscitation need) and 5 min (response to resuscitation). <7 at 5 min → resuscitate + reassess.
